title: Relevant Context-Load as a Saturating Novelty Knob in Language Models: A Cross-Family Study
description: Does the amount of relevant context in a language model's window act as a knob on the novelty of its output, and is there an optimal input-to-window ratio? Using a controlled ideation task with embedding-based novelty and groundedness metrics across four models (gemma2:9b through a frontier model), we find that (1) relevant context as neutral reference — with no instruction to be original — reliably moves ideation off the mainstream answer across every family tested (+49% to +147%), with groundedness largely preserved and an isolation design ruling out the implicit-instruction confound; (2) the effect is a saturating switch, not a tunable dial, with no optimal ratio — novelty saturates within a few hundred tokens of relevant context; and (3) this absolute threshold is scale-invariant, if anything earlier for the stronger model (~300 vs ~800 tokens). A three-judge groundedness protocol (fast LLM, strong LLM, then a blinded human tiebreaker) shows groundedness is flat across load — no coherence cost — and, as a second contribution, that automated LLM groundedness judges correlate near zero with a human rater and systematically over-rate on speculative ideation, manufacturing a spurious high-load erosion that only the human pass caught. A cautionary result for LLM-as-judge evaluation. All code, prompts, and data are released.
notes: Preprint prepared with AI assistance (Anthropic Claude) within the author's research harness; all results derive from local execution and the released data. Author is responsible for the claims.
